replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across both M70 and M73 engines. Reconditioned units are rare — specialists typically rebuild existing units. All pricing is indicative; actual costs vary by mileage, condition and workshop location.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an 8 Series V12 engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent BMW specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The V12's engine bay is tight; engine-out work is often required for major repairs.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth it on a vehicle worth £10,000+
Replacement often £4,000–£7,000 — assess vehicle value carefully

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci? Check These First
buyIf: * M73 (post-1994) — the more reliable and refined V12
* M70 with documented distributor cap and rotor history
* Full service history with regular oil changes (specialist maintained)
* Cooling system proactively replaced (water pump, radiator, hoses)
avoidIf: * M70 with no distributor cap history — the risk is real and can cause starting issues
* Any evidence of coolant loss or overheating — cooling system risk
* Poor service history — the V12 is unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive problems
* Rust — E31s are valuable; body condition is as important as the engine

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 850i and 850Ci are BMW icons — the company's first V12-powered grand tourer. The M70 (1989–1994) is a pioneering but complex engine with twin ECUs and fragile distributor caps; the M73 (1994–1999) is significantly improved with single ECU and better electronics. Both are high-maintenance specialist engines. Buy the newest M73 you can afford, maintain the cooling system proactively, and enjoy one of BMW's greatest engines.

cta.label: → Compare BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any hesitation or misfire; M70/M73 V12s should start cleanly
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — pre-1994 = M70, 1994+ = M73
* Distributor Caps (M70) — Any misfire or hesitation? Caps and rotors need checking
* Cooling System — Any coolant loss? Water pump, radiator and hoses should be recent
* Intake Gaskets — Any rough idle or hunting? Vacuum leak risk
* PCV System (M73) — Any whistling or oil consumption? PCV valve is key
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
* Rust Check — E31s are prone to rust; body condition is critical

items.question: How much does a BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned BMW 8 Series V12 engine costs £4,000–£7,000 supply only, varying by generation: M70 £3,500–£6,000, M73 £4,000–£7,000. Fitting adds £1,400–£2,200. These are specialist-only replacements — the V12 requires BMW V12 expertise. A distributor cap replacement costs £400–£800.

items.question: Is the BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci reliable?
items.answer: That depends on which engine your 850 has. The M70 (1989–1994) is a pioneering V12 with twin ECUs and fragile distributor caps — it requires careful maintenance. The M73 (1994–1999) is significantly improved with single ECU, better electronics and more power. Both are high-maintenance specialist engines. Buy the M73 if you can — it's the better engine.

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci?
items.answer: M70: distributor cap and rotor wear (misfire, starting issues, £400–£800), intake manifold gasket leaks (rough idle, £800–£1,500), cooling system failure (£500–£1,500). M73: cooling system failure, intake gasket leaks, PCV/oil separator failure (whistling, oil consumption, £500–£1,200). Both: cooling system must be proactively maintained.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: 8 Series values range from £8,000–£50,000+ depending on condition, mileage and specification. A £4,000–£7,000 reconditioned engine represents 10–80% of vehicle value — our 70% BMW scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained, low-mileage example, replacement is often justified. For a higher-mileage example with other issues, assess scrap value first. The 850Ci M73 is generally more valuable and worth the investment.

items.question: How do I find my BMW 8 Series 850i / 850Ci engine code?
items.answer: The 850i/850Ci has used two engines: M70B50 (1989–1994, 296bhp) and M73B54 (1994–1999, 322bhp). The 850i badge (1989–1994) is the M70; the 850Ci badge (1994–1999) is the M73. Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

items.question: Can I repair the distributor cap issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — the distributor caps and rotors must be replaced together (£400–£800). The caps corrode internally, causing misfires and poor running. At the first sign of misfire or hesitation, book an inspection. This is the most common failure on M70 850i models. Use genuine Bosch parts. Replacing the caps and rotors restores smooth running.
